text: The robotic camera provides a view of the anastomosis site, revealing possible stricture and adhesions around the area.
The robotic camera provides a view of the anastomosis, revealing possible stricture and adhesions in the small bowel.
Four robotic ports are placed vertically in the left hemiabdomen. Upon entry, dense adhesions are visible and are sharply dissected. A perforation is observed at the site of the prior anastomosis.
Four robotic ports are placed vertically in the left hemiabdomen. Upon entry, dense adhesions are visible and are sharply dissected. A perforation is observed at the site of the previous anastomosis.
The surgeon sharply dissects dense adhesions between the colon and liver, as well as the abdominal sidewall. Additional dense interloop adhesions are also sharply dissected.

The surgeon sharply dissects dense adhesions between the colon and liver, as well as the abdominal sidewall. Additional dense interloop adhesions are also sharply dissected, revealing significant inflammation and adherence of the bowel to the mesentery.
The video shows significant inflammation with the bowel adherent to the mesentery. The surgeon uses a sponge to dissect through the interloop adhesions, separating the bowel from the mesentery.
The surgeon uses a sponge to dissect through interloop adhesions, noting a small serosal injury during the process. The injury is repaired with an interrupted 3-0 Vicryl suture.
During the dissection of interloop adhesions, a small serosal injury is identified and sutured with interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon dissects the terminal ileum free of adhesions, creating a small window in the mesentery. Bleeding is controlled using bipolar forceps, and the vessel sealer is employed to divide the small bowel mesentery.

The surgeon dissects the terminal ileum free of adhesions, creates a small window in the mesentery, and controls any bleeding using bipolar forceps and a vessel sealer. The small bowel mesentery is divided with the vessel sealer.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to retract the small bowel, creating an adequate mesenteric window. A 45mm stapler is then used to divide the terminal ileum. The vessel sealer is employed to divide the remaining small bowel mesentery down to the level of the previous anastomosis.

The robotic fourth arm retracts the small bowel to create a mesenteric window, allowing the surgeon to use a 45mm stapler to divide the terminal ileum. A vessel sealer is then used to divide the remaining small bowel mesentery down to the level of the previous anastomosis.

The surgeon sharply dissects the colon distal to the anastomosis, which is adherent to the liver and small bowel loops, using a sponge and suction. The hepatic flexure and transverse colon are carefully separated from the liver's edge, with the fourth arm and a sponge used to retract the liver.

The surgeon sharply dissects the colon distal to the anastomosis, which is adherent to the liver and small bowel loops, using a sponge and suctioning. The hepatic flexure and transverse colon are carefully separated from the liver's edge, with the fourth arm and a sponge used to retract the liver.

A window is created in the colonic mesentery five centimeters from the previous anastomosis. The colon is elevated and divided with a 45mm stapler. The remaining colonic mesentery is divided using a vessel sealer, and any adhesions between the colon and mesocolon are sharply dissected.

The surgeon creates a window in the colonic mesentery five centimeters from the previous anastomosis. The colon is elevated and divided using a 45mm stapler. The remaining colonic mesentery is divided with a vessel sealer, and adhesions between the colon and mesocolon are sharply dissected.

The surgeon frees the prior anastomosis from the mesocolon and sets it aside. Remaining adhesions are sharply lysed. The terminal ileum is flipped and positioned in the right upper quadrant alongside the transverse colon. A stay suture is placed to stabilize the anastomosis, which is retracted using the fourth arm. Monopolar scissors are used to create a colotomy and enterotomy.

The surgeon frees the prior anastomosis from the mesocolon and places it aside. Remaining adhesions are sharply lysed. The terminal ileum is flipped and positioned in the right upper quadrant alongside the transverse colon. A stay suture is placed to stabilize the anastomosis, which is retracted using the fourth arm. Monopolar scissors are used to create a colotomy and enterotomy.

The surgeon creates an isoparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis using a 45mm GIA stapler. After the anastomosis is formed, the stay suture is removed. The common channel is then closed in a canal fashion with a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture.

The surgeon creates an isoparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis using a 45mm GIA stapler. After removing the stay suture, the common channel is closed with a 3-0 absorbable barb suture in a canal fashion.
The surgeon optimizes exposure and retraction using the fourth arm, providing a clear view of the surgical field for further manipulation.

The surgeon optimizes exposure and retraction using the fourth arm. Indocyanine green (ICG) dye is applied to assess the perfusion and blood supply of the transverse colon and ileum, with visible fluorescence indicating vascular integrity.

Indocyanine green (ICG) dye is applied to assess the perfusion and blood supply of the transverse colon and ileum, revealing vascular flow patterns. The mesenteric defect is closed using a running stitch with a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using a running technique with a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
